speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interviewing Anita Karl. The date is December 10, 1995. This is the beginning of tape three. Would you please continue with what you were telling me?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they discovered this hiding place of all those-- that group of Jews. And instead of making a large hole for them to come out easily, their diabolical plan was to make it as small as possible to make it very difficult for them. And so they pulled out each head that appeared by the hair, taking off their skin and the hair together of every man, woman, and child that came out. It was a horrible sight. It was so close to where we were standing and not being able to do anything. My mother left the house. She wanted to see if there was a way of helping them. But they were all put-- they were like 80 of them, more or less, with little children. Men, women, women that were pregnant, women that had babies in their arms. Each and every one of the adults were put against the wall, and each baby that was not able to walk was taken by the feet and hit against the wall, like you would do with a fly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Swatter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Swatter, until they were all dead. They were shot right then and there. One of the women had a little bundle in her arms, and she saw my mother, so she kicked the bundle towards her. But the German saw it, and before my mother could move, he grabbed the bundle. He took the little baby that must have been maybe a week old and ripped it apart by the legs in half. Such cruelty. Such inhuman-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Such inhumanity from one person to another. It is unfathomable. I cannot today believe that a person is able to perform this cruel act on another human being. And these were already a group of people that most of them have studied. They were the intelligentsia of Europe. They were superiors to everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: They felt they were educated. The Germans were a higher class of people. And this is how they showed it? This was-- to which extent their cruelty went is unbelievable. Unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: The experiments they did, tying pregnant women's legs just to see how she would suffer without being able to give birth until she burst, to experiments with twins, to massacring people or burning them in the ovens. It is unbelievable what went on. And the world looked on, and the world looked on.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one lifted a finger to help us. No one heard our cries. No one came to-- to-- to resolve this and to save us. Where was God? Where were the other people? Where was God looking down at us and seeing what was done to their-- to his children, to the chosen people that he chose? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit more time went by. My mother was, since she had no skills baking, walking miles from one village to others, selling her goods so we could eat, and so we could have something to wear. My father was hidden in the-- in the cave, in that cave. And [SIGHS] news were very bad. There were no news. We were not in touch with anybody. We didn't know if by some miracle someone had survived. And time went on slowly, and for us there was no end to-- to-- to these horrors and to these times of-- of pain. And-- and-- and the only moments of peace that I personally found was in the church, was when the holidays came. Although I didn't know much about it, and my mother didn't know what to do when Easter came or the other holidays that were observed, we made do, and she pushed us on, cared for us, and gave us the best of whatever was available at that time, which was very little. We had added to our staple of bread, black bread and water, we have a little bit of milk and a little bit-- we had a cow, so we had a little bit of milk. The woman who cared for us while my mother was going from town to town selling her goods would feed us, would take care of us, in a way, and would tell me also, if you don't eat, the Jew is going to get you. So I grew up fearing the Jews, thinking they were some kind of monsters, inhuman, and that this was the way life was. And in this fear, our time of a year and a half finished in Buczacz. And we-- there were rumors that the Russians were coming, and the Germans were retreated. Retreating. At that time, Buczacz, Lwow, and all the surrounding cities were Judenfrei, which is free of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no more Jews to be found in any of that area. They had all been either killed or deported to the larger camps for the extermination. They were all put into ovens. They were all killed. From our family, we did not know anything. But one day, the Germans came in and said, you all have to leave because the Russians are coming, and we are burning the village down. You may not stay here any longer. So my mother gathered a few things, and figured if the war is almost over, if the Russians are coming, we will soon be able to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who knows? It might be the reverse. The Germans might win, defeat the Russians. So she knew that there were Jews hiding in the forests. We were surrounding-- surrounded by beautiful, thick forest. And she decided that the best thing to do was to go and contact the partisans and take my father and the children into the woods, and save ourselves in the woods. So about that was the first plan. But then she found out that the partisans would not take the children. They will only take her and my father and the children would have to stay with this [INAUDIBLE] Christian woman, with a Gentile woman, and maybe survive or maybe not. So they sat together, my father and my mother, and planned what to do. How do you decide if to save the husband or if to save your children? And this terrible decision my mother didn't not know how to affront, she said. From all the horrible decisions she had made, and all the wars she had fought, she did not know what to do. She was very much in love with her father-- with her husband. She loved him dearly, as every wife would, but she loved her children too. And this is a decision no woman should have to make. Who do you sacrifice-- the children or the husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the end, my father made the decision easy on her, and he said, Malca, I have lived my life. I have been a good husband. I have been a good father. God will help me. I am already 33 years old, after all, but the children are just starting. Let them live. Let them be a testimony, a living testimony to what happened to us here. Let them tell the world someday so this would never happen again. Let the world listen to them, to my children. You save them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she decided to hide my father in the cave.Tell him not to come out for no reason. And it would not last more than a week. In a week, she would come back. And in the meantime, she would take the children with all those people that were leaving, they were to leave the Buczacz area, the town, the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we walked for miles and miles and miles, until we got to the next village and the next village. And two days passed by, and my mother couldn't live with herself. She just could not stand the idea of having left him behind. She wanted to do something more. She wanted to go back and save him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she left the three children with a neighbor, and she said, I must go back because I have too many things. Winter is coming-- this was June-- winter is coming. How am I going to face it? I have left all the clothes. In the rush, they didn't let me carry. And I will be back in two days. So she ran back to our house. But the town was already surrounded by the Germans, and nobody was able to walk in. And a German officer came to her and asked her, what are you looking for? So my mother said, my house is over there, and I have left the children's clothes. I want to take them with me because it is cold, the winter will come, and I have nothing for them. So the German said, OK, but I'll tell you what. I'll go with you. I will go with you because if you go alone, they will not let you in. So the officer went with my mother into the house, and he says to her, in the meantime, your house, because it's on the hill, has become the [? center ?] of operations for the Germans, so we have taken over your house. But we have not touched your clothes, so you will go in and take everything. As they were approaching the house, and my mother was going to go in and look in the back to see if all was well, a German comes out, a soldier. And he asks, who is this woman? So the officer says, she has lived here. This is her house, and she's come to pick up the clothes for her children. She'll just be in and out. So he says, or is she coming looking for the Jew we found here in the backyard yesterday? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my mother, that was the end of her courage, that was the end of her wisdom. Of-- of-- she was so distraught, she couldn't say a word. So the German-- the officer says, look what happened. The woman has not been here for two days. What happened in her absence, it is not-- none of her concern. She came here with me for her things. Let her get in, get her things, and I'll take her back. So God was watching over us after all. In a way, he was there. He took the-- he told my mother to go in. My mother went in, grabbed her things. [CRYING] I'm sorry. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took her things, and she didn't know what she was taking. She took everything she could in a bag and left the house. As she left with the officer, the officer-- she was speechless. She couldn't talk. She couldn't say anything. The officer took her to the gate, to the entrance of the city, and he told her go. Go, and don't come back here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As she walked, she said she cannot leave my father. She must go and see where he is. She has to find out if he's still alive. Maybe another jewel, maybe something else that she had still left would help. She had a golden watch, a pocket watch of my father and grandfather. My grandfather had given it to him when he was bar mitzvahed, and my father gave it to my mother as his last souvenir. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And she thought she would take this watch and trade it for his life. So she asked where are those Jews that were found here? So they said to her, they are in the jail. There about 30 of them in jail. The last ones that they were able to find that were hiding and running. And so she went to the jail. She run up to the jail, and a man was coming out of the jail, and she asked him, where are the Jews? Do you know anything about them? And he said, oh the Jews. They were all shot this morning. There is no one left. There is no one left. You can go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so with this pain, this terrible pain of knowing now for sure that her husband was dead, and my father had died two weeks before the Russians came, just a few days. He had survived all of these years, and just a few days he was found. And he was found by such a coincidence that it was unimaginable. The German in the house was calling another one was shaving by the cave where my father was hidden. And he said to him, [GERMAN], come out. I know you are in there. Come out. My father thought they were calling him. That they had found him. And with his nerves destroyed, and with the anger and with the pain, he came out. He thought they were calling him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is how I lost my father. He was shot. And even though I knew that he was shot, I have always looked for him. I have always searched for him. I have never, never given up. [CRYING] I still think that maybe somehow he was able to escape. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When my son was-- my son Sammy turned, last month, 29 years old, so I gave him the watch. So he has it. Because he has his name. He's the namesake of my father. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It is not easy, as you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] break down once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's-- it's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother joined us, rejoined us, and we made our way to Krakow. We arrived in Krakow when the-- Schindler's Jews were leaving. We came in still as Aryans, still afraid to say we are Jews, still thinking maybe there were no more Jews left alive. Maybe they were all killed, and we were the last Jews, and we would be in a museum soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother didn't dare to say or tell us, and we continued. The war was over. The Germans had left Poland. We continued living as Gentiles for a little while. But soon, my mother had to make another decision. Was it fair to the children to tell them that we were Jews one day and be exposed to another persecution, to another Holocaust? Or would it be better to stay as Gentiles and raise the children, who had already lived in, into this situation where [? use ?] did not know they were Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the blood of her ancestors and all her family was clamoring to her not to do that. She had to give us back our heritage. And although I cried bitter tears when I found out, and she told me, you are a Jew. You are not a Gentile. I didn't want to. I said, I don't want to be. They will kill me. They will come after me one day. I want to go to church. I want to be a Gentile. I don't want to be that. And slowly, with much gentleness and care and love, she gave me my Jewishness back to me. And she gave me my traditions and my roots and my history and my origins. And I love them dearly, and I have to become a very devoted Jew. And caring and wanting to educate others about the Holocaust and about what happened to us. And about the bravery of some people, like my mother, who is, I am sure, an unsung hero of our people. Who did so much to save us, and through us give testimony of the accounts that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So although now we are a living testimony, one day we will be a page in the history book. And so we must remember, and we must tell the stories. We lived in Krakow for several months, and my mother met a friend of her sister's who survived, and she helped her with the children and planned. And-- and she had news that in Berlin, the Americans had occupied and came in, and they were helping out the survivors through different refugee camps, and that it would be to our advantage to get there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made our way with her to the Russian border, but the Russians were not letting anybody out from Poland. They wanted the people to stay in Poland. They needed the people. And they were dividing Poland at that time, and they were dividing Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So no one was allowed to go on the other side. And my mother said, well, we will manage. We will cross in December, in the middle of the snow, at night. Nobody will see us. We came so far, we'll go a little further. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Just before we did that, a nephew of my mother appeared, a son of one of her brothers. And she was so happy to see him. He was [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He immigrated, was able to go to-- to Palestine and smuggle himself, create a family, found Yad Vashem, one of the founders of Yad Vashem. And shortly, he died. It was too much for him after all of that to work in the Yad Vashem. It was very difficult for him. And she also found a niece and then another nephew, the children of her sister, her eldest sister. And they are in this-- the sister, my only cousin, still lives in Israel, in Haifa at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Salsza, Salsza Hilner. And Mundek Brandt Junior. He just died, just passed away. And Israel Brandt. Rebekah. They all-- some of them were there before the war, some of them came after. But only one lives today, Salsza Hilner, of Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Berlin where you met them, or was this before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buczacz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Buczacz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Krakow. We found them coincidentally. Big coincidence. Because at that time, nobody knew where you are, who you are. Are you-- this friend of my mother entered into a drugstore, and the girl that was helping her started a conversation with her. And she says, what city are you from? So she says, I'm from Lwow. And the friend of my mother said, you know, I have here a friend of mine with three children. She's also for-- from Lwow. Maybe you know her. So they gave the names, and it-- it was the niece who had survived in the-- with the partisans and my mother, and it was like having a little bit of my mother's sister with her. And that's what happened with the other. The other-- but they knew, they saw that everybody was murdered and killed, so they brought news that there was no hope in searching for anybody or going back to Lwow, because it was all destroyed. It was all-- There was nothing. No one to-- to try to find. But in Berlin, the Red Cross was trying to find people. And so they were going after names, trying to. And when we decided to go to Berlin and cross over the border, the Russian border, we were caught. So that was another terrible moment for my mother, because she said, again, now the Russians will take us to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain to me what that experience was like in more detail when they caught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were-- at night, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was at night. We were trying-- my mother's friend, the three children, and my mother-- trying to cross over the railroad tracks, or the border, what it was. But at that very moment, there was a group of Russians who had gone out, because they didn't care much. The Russians were away from home, there was no more war, Berlin was half Russian anyway, so they were sitting there in their hut, just doing like this. So it was not that difficult. But still, it was dangerous. Because if they caught you, they would send you back to Russia. They also had camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us in, into their hut. They caught us, and they took us in. So the children, we were crying, scared, afraid. I was besides myself. I don't want to be caught. I don't want to be shot. Because caught and shot and killed and blood was the same thing to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German said, oh, don't worry, don't worry. And he brought us chocolate, which we hadn't seen-- I had not known what chocolate was, because I couldn't remember from five years before. And he gave us hot milk. They were very good. The Russians, mean or bad or whatever their ideas are, they were very good to the children. The children were never to be harmed. Through all their wars and through all their massacres, and through all their whatevers, children were always protected. They were the privileged of-- of the people. So they cared for-- the contrary of the Germans. We were-- I was afraid of them because they wore a uniform. I couldn't see somebody without-- within a uniform without going-- getting hysterical, practically. So then after a little while, my mother said, OK, Junior needs to go to the bathroom. And of course, if one goes to the bathroom, we all go to the bathroom, because nobody wanted to stay alone there with the-- with the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went. What was it to go to the bathroom? To go in the snow outside the hut. My mother grabbed me, and she said, run! So I said, I'm not running. I'm not running. You run, I'm not going. I'm not running at all. And I run back to the hut, so she had no choice but to come back again.
